title: OPEC+ emergency cut of 2 million barrels per day sends Brent crude above $105
topicContext: EUR/USD is sensitive to energy price shocks because Europe is a net energy importer while the U.S. has become a net energy exporter. A sustained oil price spike acts as a terms-of-trade shock that widens Europe's current account deficit, weighs on European growth expectations, and forces the ECB into a difficult choice between fighting imported inflation and supporting a slowing economy. The dollar typically strengthens in energy crises as a safe-haven currency and because U.S. energy self-sufficiency insulates its economy relative to Europe and Asia.
body:
OPEC+ announced an emergency production cut of 2 million barrels per day on Monday, the largest single reduction since the pandemic-era cuts of 2020, citing 'deteriorating demand forecasts and a need to stabilize prices at levels that support long-term investment in production capacity.'

Brent crude jumped 8.2% to $105.40 per barrel immediately after the announcement, its highest level since March 2023. West Texas Intermediate rose 7.8% to $101.20. Energy stocks rallied globally, with the STOXX Europe 600 Oil & Gas index up 4.1%.

The cut caught markets off guard. Most analysts had expected OPEC+ to maintain current production levels at this week's regular meeting. Saudi Arabia's energy minister said the decision reflected 'proactive market management' and denied it was a response to pressure from any member state. Russia's deputy prime minister said Moscow fully supported the decision.

The move comes at a sensitive time for the global economy. Central banks in both the U.S. and Europe are already battling above-target inflation, and a sustained rise in oil prices risks reigniting price pressures just as they appeared to be moderating. European natural gas futures also rose sharply on the news, up 6.3%, as traders priced in broader energy supply tightening.

The euro fell 0.9% against the dollar in the hours after the announcement, reflecting Europe's vulnerability to energy price shocks as a major net importer. Analysts at several banks lowered their short-term EUR/USD forecasts, with one noting that 'a sustained move in Brent above $100 is worth roughly 150 basis points of additional imported inflation for the eurozone, which puts the ECB in an impossible position.'

U.S. President Biden said the administration was 'monitoring the situation closely' and would 'consider all options' including a release from the Strategic Petroleum Reserve. The White House declined to comment on whether the decision had been discussed in advance with Saudi officials.

Consumer-facing sectors sold off on both sides of the Atlantic, with airlines, trucking companies, and chemical manufacturers among the worst performers. European auto stocks fell 2.4% on expectations of higher input costs.
